description: Contingency decal stacks — the small grids of sponsor logos that line a car's quarter panel, b-pillar, or sidepod. Used by SPB to suggest aligned sponsor block placement.

schema.name: string — human label
schema.typical_count: [min, max] — how many logos usually fit
schema.size_hint_uv: [width, height] of each logo cell in UV space

stacks.name: NASCAR B-Pillar Stack
stacks.notes: Classic NASCAR contingency tower — 6 to 12 small horizontal-format logos stacked top to bottom. Each row is roughly 1.8% of UV height. Logos must be sized identically for visual cleanliness even when source aspect ratios differ.

stacks.name: NASCAR Quarter Panel Cluster
stacks.notes: Horizontal sponsor row that typically runs above the quarter-panel sweep. Larger than the b-pillar stack because there is more horizontal canvas. Often paired with primary sponsor block above it.

stacks.name: GT3 Endurance Stack
stacks.notes: 2-column grid common in 24h endurance racing. Slightly smaller logos than NASCAR. Allows more sponsors per square cm at the cost of some readability from distance.

stacks.name: LMP Acrylic Sponsor Panel
stacks.notes: Prototype cars often have a translucent acrylic panel reserved for sponsors. Layout is grid-based, with logos sized to fill the available rectangle. Backlit at night events — logos should remain readable when illuminated from behind.

stacks.name: F1 Floor Edge Strip
stacks.notes: Long, low-profile horizontal strip that fits the narrow band along the floor edge. Wide aspect ratio — logos are stretched to fit horizontally. Visible from below in onboard helicam shots.

stacks.name: IndyCar Sidepod Stack
stacks.notes: Vertical stack on the side of the sidepod, typically below the primary sponsor. Camera-facing during oval racing telecasts. Slightly larger than NASCAR b-pillar logos because of viewing distance from infield cameras.

stacks.name: Dirt Late Model Quarter Panel
stacks.notes: Larger logos with generous spacing — viewing distance is short but dust is heavy. Few but visible logos beat many tiny ones. Often hand-applied vinyl rather than pre-printed wraps.

stacks.name: Vintage Lower Door Strip
stacks.notes: Period-correct sponsor strip in a single horizontal row. Generous spacing reflects the era — no team had 12 sponsors. Logos were often hand-painted rather than vinyl, so allow slight irregularity in the alignment.
